steps.prompt: Which word is a relative pronoun?

hints.suam: 'Suam' is a possessive adjective meaning 'his own'

hints.to fly: That would be 'volare' — 'voluerit' comes from 'velle' (to wish)
hints.to roll: That would be 'volvere' — 'voluerit' comes from 'velle' (to wish)
hints.to be strong: That would be 'valere' — 'voluerit' comes from 'velle' (to wish)

steps.reveal: voluerit = shall have wished (perf. subj., in a relative clause of characteristic)

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect indicative would be 'voluit' — 'voluerit' in a generalizing relative clause is subjunctive
hints.future perfect indicative: Future perfect looks identical, but the generalizing 'Qui' calls for subjunctive
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'velit' — 'voluerit' is perfect subjunctive

hints.nolo, nolle, nolui, —: These are the parts of 'nolle' (to not wish)
hints.malo, malle, malui, —: These are the parts of 'malle' (to prefer)
hints.valeo, valere, valui, valitum: These are the parts of 'valere' (to be strong/well)

hints.to seek: That would be 'petere/quaerere' — 'perdet' comes from 'perdere' (to lose)
hints.to pursue: That would be 'persequi' — 'perdet' comes from 'perdere' (to lose)
hints.to sell: That would be 'vendere' — 'perdet' comes from 'perdere' (to lose)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'perdit' — 'perdet' with -e- is future (3rd conjugation)
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'perdat' — 'perdet' is future indicative

hints.peto, petere, petivi, petitum: These are the parts of 'petere' (to seek)
hints.pendo, pendere, pependi, pensum: These are the parts of 'pendere' (to weigh/pay)
hints.credo, credere, credidi, creditum: These are the parts of 'credere' (to believe)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'anima' — 'animam' with -am is accusative

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'animae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'animae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'animae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: What does 'salvam facere' mean as a phrase?

hints.to sacrifice: That would be 'sacrificare' — 'salvam facere' means 'to make safe/save'
hints.to set free: That would be 'liberare' — 'salvam facere' literally means 'to make safe'
hints.to make holy: That would be 'sanctificare' — 'salvam facere' means 'to save'

translation: Whoever wishes to save his life will lose it
